title: SOUNDS ABOUT RIGHT
subtitle: For questions 1–8, read the text below and think of the word that best fits each space. Use only one word in each space. There is an example at the beginning (0).
passage:
Getting the right tone in restaurant design is (0) NO mean feat since it is such a sensory experience. While restaurant design primarily tries to emulate or reflect the taste of the cuisine, as the food is what is centre [[1]], this is shortly followed by sight to get that all important wow factor, especially in high-end establishments. Invariably the most frequently overlooked sense is that of sound. [[2]] been subjected to all sorts of soundscapes in restaurants, many of them unpleasant, a growing number of diners now regard the volume and type of background noise as a fundamental consideration when deciding [[3]] or not to make a reservation.

[[4]] to popular belief, music is deemed less intrusive by diners than ambient noise. Silence in a restaurant can make people feel ill at [[5]], so restaurants often splash out [[6]] sound equipment to create the right ambiance. However, it is the noise from the diners that impacts the soundscape the most, therefore when assessing the acoustic properties of a restaurant space, it is essential to [[7]] this in mind. In addition, materials can play a vital role in that installing acoustic wall panelling, carpets and velvet upholstery count [[8]] reducing the noise footprint of a restaurant since they all possess sound absorbency properties.

explanations.1: Centre stage is a fixed phrase: the food is the main focus — what everything else revolves around.
explanations.2: Having been subjected to… opens a perfect participle clause: diners experienced unpleasant soundscapes before forming this view.
explanations.3: Whether or not is the standard pairing when you introduce a binary choice (to book or not).
explanations.4: Contrary to popular belief is a set expression introducing a view that contradicts what people usually assume.
explanations.5: Ill at ease is an idiom: uncomfortable or awkward — silence can create that feeling.
explanations.6: Splash out on (something) is the correct dependent preposition: spend a lot of money on equipment.
explanations.7: Keep in mind and bear in mind are both fixed; you must remember or take account of this when assessing acoustics.
explanations.8: Count towards (doing) something means help achieve or contribute to an outcome — here, reducing the noise footprint.
